nutrient_source.title: Fish, croaker, Atlantic, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA data for mustard greens (preserved/pickled) and cooking oil for composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Yellow Croaker Soup with Preserved Greens exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for Atlantic croaker (raw, FDC ID 171958), pickled mustard greens, and cooking oil, adjusted for pan-frying and simmering cooking losses and dilution from broth. The input AI-generated values (protein 9.5g, fat 4.5g, carbohydrate 1.5g, energy 85 kcal per 100g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored composite estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 450mg/100g reflects the high salt content of preserved greens and is consistent with published data on similar preserved-vegetable fish soups. All values represent the whole dish including broth.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fish-based dishes and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fish and protein-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yellow Croaker Soup with Preserved Greens. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for high-protein, low-carbohydrate fish dishes. The dish contains only 1.5g carbohydrate per 100g; the dominant macronutrients are protein (9.5g) and fat (4.5g), which strongly attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per 400g serving = (15 × 6.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.9, classified as very low. This estimate is consistent with the clinical literature on protein-fat dominant fish soups.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450mg/100g; ~1800mg per 400g serving) from preserved mustard greens and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to a small serving and the salty broth should not be fully consumed, as a full 400g serving provides approximately 1800mg sodium — 78% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.5g/100g) and high-quality fish protein with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yellow Croaker Soup with Preserved Greens is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a main dish component, given its very low carbohydrate load (6g per 400g serving), low estimated GI (~15), and high-quality lean fish protein; however, the high sodium content requires monitoring in patients with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from yellow croaker fish offset by high sodium from preserved greens
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yellow croaker provides beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids (estimated 1.4g/100g) including omega-3s that support cardiovascular health, but the very high sodium content (450mg/100g) is a significant concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consumption should be limited to small portions with reduced broth intake, and lower-sodium preparation methods should be considered.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from fish beneficial, but preserved vegetables may be high in histamine and sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids and Vitamin D from yellow croaker may support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis, but preserved mustard greens are high in histamine and sodium, which may trigger flares in sensitive individuals; patients should assess personal tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellow Croaker Soup with Preserved Greens (咸菜黄鱼汤, Xián cài huángyú tāng; also known as 大汤黄鱼, Dàtāng huángyú) is a celebrated signature dish of Ningbo cuisine, originating from Ningbo, a major coastal port city in Zhejiang Province, eastern China. The dish showcases two defining ingredients of the Ningbo culinary tradition: fresh large yellow croaker (大黄鱼, Larimichthys crocea), a prized marine fish with tender, sweet, and delicate white flesh, and 'Xue Li Hong' (雪里红), a variety of potherb mustard that is salt-preserved to produce a salty, tangy, and crunchy condiment. The preparation begins with the yellow croaker being pan-fried in oil until the skin turns golden, a step that triggers protein and fat emulsification during subsequent simmering, producing the dish's characteristic milky-white, rich broth. The preserved greens are then added and the dish is simmered together, infusing the broth with sharp acidity, umami depth, and savory complexity. The resulting soup is simultaneously refreshing and intensely satisfying, with a clean fish sweetness balanced by the piquant preserved vegetable. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (~9.5g/100g), moderate in fat (~4.5g/100g including beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids), very low in carbohydrates (~1.5g/100g), and provides meaningful amounts of Vitamin D (1.5mcg/100g), Vitamin B12 (1.2mcg/100g), selenium (12.5mcg/100g), and phosphorus (85mg/100g). The primary clinical concern is its very high sodium content (~450mg/100g), attributable to the salt-preserved greens, making it unsuitable for individuals on strict low-sodium diets. The dish is a staple of Ningbo household cooking and is prominently featured in Zhejiang restaurant menus as a regional specialty centerpiece.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Yellow Croaker Soup with Preserved Greens exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for Atlantic croaker and pickled mustard greens, adjusted for cooking method and dilution. The AI-generated input values are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for protein-dominant fish dishes. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Atlantic croaker, raw, FDC ID 171958; pickled mustard greens) adjusted for pan-frying and simmering, as no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific Ningbo dish. The input AI-generated values are consistent with this estimate and have been retained. 2. Saturated fat (0.8g), monounsaturated fat (1.6g), and polyunsaturated fat (1.4g) are estimated from USDA croaker and cooking oil data; these values carry medium confidence. 3. Cholesterol (35mg/100g) is estimated from USDA croaker data adjusted for dilution in soup. 4. Vitamin A is reported as 7.5 mcg RAE (converted from the input value of 25 mcg which likely represented retinol activity equivalents from a mixed fish/vegetable source). 5. water_g is set to -1: detailed moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026. 6. The dietary_tags 'vegetarian' and 'vegan' present in the input cultural data have been removed as this dish contains fish and is neither vegetarian nor vegan. 7. The duplicate portion entry in the input has been deduplicated to a single 400g serving. 8. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database protein-dominant fish dish data; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per 400g serving = (15 × 6.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.9 (very low). 9. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315595 is used as the closest defensible approximate anchor for 'fish soup food product'; no exact FoodOn class exists for this dish. FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the closest defensible approximate anchor for fish-based soups.
